title: Chromolaena ascendens
taxonomy: Chromolaena ascendens is classified as a member of the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. It belongs to the tribe Eupatorieae, a group of plants known for their showy flowers and often aromatic leaves. Chromolaena ascendens is also classified as a species within the genus Chromolaena, which consists of flowering plants native to the Americas. The genus Chromolaena is further classified within the subfamily Eupatorioideae, a subfamily of flowering plants characterized by their composite flower heads. This classification is based on the plant's morphology, anatomy, and molecular characteristics.
uses: Chromolaena ascendens has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its extracts are said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making it useful in treating wounds and infections. The plant is also believed to have antiseptic and antifungal properties, which can help in preventing the growth of microorganisms. In some cultures, it is used to treat fever and other respiratory issues. Additionally, its leaves are used to treat various skin conditions and allergies due to its soothing effects.

side_effects: Chromolaena ascendens, like other plants in the Sutherlandia genus, may cause side effects such as gastrointestinal issues and allergic reactions when ingested. Some users have reported experiencing nausea, diarrhea, and stomach pain after consuming the plant. Chromolaena ascendens may also interact with certain medications, including blood thinners and diabetes medications. The plant's use may lead to an imbalance of electrolytes, particularly potassium, in the body. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of Chromolaena ascendens.
intro: Chromolaena ascendens, also known as "Piedra del Cielo" or "Cromolaena," is a plant species within the family Asteraceae, commonly found in the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and skin conditions, due to its numerous health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Chromolaena ascendens include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Chromolaena ascendens is often prepared as a tea, infusion, or decoction, and is also used in the production of herbal remedies, such as tonics and ointments, to treat various health conditions.
